findings.fact: UltraViolet, the studio-consortium 'rights locker' run by DECE, announced closure in January 2019 and shut down July 31, 2019; DECE president Wendy Aylsworth framed it as a response to the market shifting toward streaming/subscription.
At closure it had over 30 million user accounts and 300+ million movies/shows registered in cloud libraries — large registration numbers that still did not translate into a sustainable ecosystem.

The central 'Coordinator' (built and operated by Neustar) stored only rights records and user data, never the content files; retailers integrated against it, and a Common File Format was specced so one download could play across multiple approved DRM systems. Spec development with the ~90-member consortium took about 2.5 years before launch.

Three compounding causes: Disney never joined and instead launched the competing Disney Movies Anywhere; the dominant retail platforms (Apple/iTunes, Amazon, Google) refused to integrate; and early consumer reception was poor due to clunky account-linking and DRM friction. By 2018 Fox, Universal and Lionsgate had stopped putting new releases on it.

Because the Coordinator held rights records rather than files, users who linked retailer accounts before shutdown kept access to their movies at those retailers; users who unlinked early lost their collections. The registry/content separation allowed a mostly non-destructive wind-down.

Movies Anywhere is owned and operated by Disney (launched Feb 25, 2014 as Disney Movies Anywhere; rebranded and opened to other studios Oct 12, 2017). It runs on Disney's internal KeyChest rights-synchronization technology: a centralized cloud locker that queries purchases across linked retailer accounts (iTunes, Amazon, Google TV, Fandango at Home, etc.) and mirrors entitlements across all of them.

As of mid-2026 it is the surviving interoperability layer for purchased film: roughly 14.5 million active users averaging ~30 films each, and nearly 10,000 titles across five majors after Lionsgate finally joined in June 2026 (225 films initially, ~100/month thereafter). Paramount and MGM still do not participate.

findings.topic: Movies Anywhere — fragility of voluntary participation
findings.fact: Participation is revocable at will: Google abruptly withdrew retailer support on Oct 31, 2025 (reportedly collateral damage from a YouTube TV–Disney carriage dispute) and took over a month to restore it; Microsoft Movies & TV withdrew in 2025. Users' cross-platform access depends on bilateral business relationships they cannot see or control.

Founded January 2018 by Brian Fargo, Robot Cache was a blockchain-branded PC game store promising resale of digital games: publishers kept 95% on new sales (vs Steam's ~70%), and on resales 70% went to the publisher, 25% back to the seller (paid in its IRON utility currency, also earnable via crypto mining), 5% to Robot Cache. It raised ~$10.5M, converted a planned $15M ICO into a Reg-D securities offering after regulatory shifts, and eventually abandoned issuing its own cryptocurrency.

Open beta launched May 12, 2020; an investor report showed only $532 in game-software revenue for all of 2024 against an accumulated deficit exceeding $30M. The company renamed itself Cloudhands, Inc. (AI marketplace pivot) on March 18, 2025, and the store closed May 4, 2026.

findings.topic: Robot Cache — custodial reality behind blockchain branding
findings.fact: Despite 'blockchain-powered' marketing, ownership was entirely platform-dependent: after shutdown, users can no longer play purchased games even if fully downloaded, because the client/DRM requires the service. The blockchain layer provided no survivability for the licenses it supposedly anchored.
findings.source: https://massivelyop.com/2026/04/06/blockchain-powered-digital-storefront-robot-cache-is-closing-and-yoinking-users-games/ (Massively Overpowered, Apr 6 2026; corroborated by PC Gamer)

findings.fact: Launched July 2022 on Ethereum L2 rails, with an Immutable X partnership and a joint $100M developer grant fund; total volume was ~$29M from ~24,000 traders by October 2022 (first-week volume ~$7M, then rapid decay). GameStop shut its crypto wallet in late 2023 and closed the marketplace on Feb 2, 2024, publicly blaming 'continuing regulatory uncertainty' in crypto.

findings.topic: GameStop NFT — asset survivability vs utility
findings.fact: Because the NFTs were genuinely on-chain (not custodial database rows), they remained accessible and tradeable on other marketplaces after GameStop's exit — the one structural win of the open-token model. But the assets' practical utility and liquidity largely died with the venue that marketed them.

Ubisoft Quartz (announced Dec 9, 2021) put 'Digits' — playable cosmetic NFTs on Tezos, with open-sourced smart contracts — into Ghost Recon Breakpoint on PC. Reception was hostile: the announcement video was mass-disliked and delisted, and early secondary-market activity totaled roughly $400. In April 2022 Ubisoft ended Breakpoint content development, ending new Digits; tokens persist on Tezos but the only game that rendered them is frozen.

Ubisoft did not formally 'shut down' Quartz; it quietly stopped, and by 2025-2026 the company had pulled back from in-game NFT economies in flagship titles toward small, isolated web3 pilots — consistent with a broader AAA retreat from embedding token ownership in mainline games.

Book.io (Cardano-first, now multichain across Cardano, Ethereum, Polygon, Algorand) sells ebooks/audiobooks as 'Decentralized Encrypted Assets' where the token gates access to the encrypted book, enabling resale with author/publisher royalties on secondary sales. Vendor-reported numbers: 130,000+ tokens sold across 70+ titles. Still alive in 2026 but tiny (~12 employees per Tracxn, Feb 2026; latest funding an angel round Apr 2025); it expanded via a consumer brand Stuff.io (e.g., a collectible Battlefield Earth ebook, Feb 2026) and a Cardano Foundation pilot distributing 2,000 on-chain copies of a book at WEF 2025. Adoption claims come from promotional/ecosystem sources, not independent audits.

Royal.io raised ~$71M (2021, a16z-backed) to sell fractional streaming-royalty shares of songs as NFTs (Nas, The Chainsmokers participated). It shut its marketplace in late 2024 after secondary volume collapsed; tokens remain tradeable on OpenSea and royalty claims continued via its site. The structural killer was securities law: fractional royalty tokens sold to retail cleanly satisfy all four Howey-test prongs, and SEC enforcement signals in 2022-2023 made the model untenable — with the legal risk asymmetrically landing on the issuing artists.

Sound.xyz (raised $20M, a16z, 2023) saw NFT mint volumes crater, pivoted in 2024 to 'Sound Premium' — a subscription fan-support product using crypto rails invisibly — and announced the legacy NFT platform's shutdown in December 2025. On-chain collections persist and artists can still claim funds from Splits contracts, again showing token persistence outliving platform utility.

findings.topic: OneOf and other music NFT deaths
findings.fact: OneOf ($63M raise, Tezos, Quincy Jones as advisor) collapsed 2023-2024 because its signed artists had no crypto-native buying audience and the ecosystem couldn't manufacture one; Mint Songs, YellowHeart and smaller platforms wound down in the same window as marketplace fees on trace volumes couldn't sustain operations. Over $300M of VC went into music NFT/web3 in 2021-2022; overall NFT trading volume fell 90%+ from 2022 peaks.

Survivors avoided speculation framing: Catalog (1/1 collectible record drops, $1K-$20K per drop), fan-club tokens explicitly marketed as membership not investment, and subscription products hiding crypto rails. The 'boring' non-crypto incumbents (Bandcamp, Patreon) outperformed every tokenized-ownership model on revenue per superfan.

Stockholm-based Anotherblock still operates in 2025: it tokenized its 500th major-label track and reports over $2.5M in royalties paid to holders in 2025, but it offloaded the regulated royalty-distribution function to RWA platform Polytrade (announced Sep 23, 2024) — surviving partly by pushing the compliance-heavy layer to a specialist.

The most funded new entrant reframes the problem as B2B IP infrastructure rather than consumer purchases: Story launched a purpose-built Layer-1 'IP blockchain' on Feb 13, 2025 with the $IP token, offering an on-chain IP registry plus a Programmable IP License (PIL) grounded in US copyright law that encodes remix rights, royalty splits and commercial permissions in smart contracts — explicitly targeting AI-era licensing of creative works.

Effective Jan 1, 2025, California bars digital storefronts from using 'buy'/'purchase' language for revocable-license goods (games, ebooks, movies, apps) unless they obtain affirmative acknowledgment or conspicuously disclose that the sale is a license — legally codifying that mainstream 'purchases' are not ownership.

The 'Stop Killing Games' European Citizens' Initiative passed 1M signatures on July 3, 2025 and closed July 31, 2025 with ~1.45M, demanding publishers provide end-of-life plans so games remain playable after server shutdowns — evidence that consumer anger about revocable licenses is politically real even where purchase behavior on 'ownership' platforms stayed weak.

Amazon removed the Kindle 'Download & Transfer via USB' feature on Feb 26, 2025, eliminating the last practical way for customers to keep local backups of purchased ebooks and intensifying the ownership-vs-license grievance the earlier ventures tried to monetize.

findings.topic: Recurring lesson — rights-holder participation is the binding constraint
findings.fact: Every failure traces first to content-side gatekeepers: UltraViolet died from Disney's refusal plus studio defection; Robot Cache's generous 70% publisher cut on resales still couldn't win major catalogs; Movies Anywhere works only because the most powerful rights holder owns it. Consumer-facing tech choices (blockchain or not) never compensated for missing catalog.
findings.source: Synthesis of TechCrunch/Engadget (2019), PC Gamer (2018-2026), Wikipedia Movies Anywhere — all cited above

findings.topic: Recurring lesson — token persistence is not license persistence
findings.fact: In every blockchain case (GameStop, Ubisoft Digits, Sound.xyz, Royal), on-chain tokens outlived the platform, but the thing users actually valued — playable game, usable cosmetic, royalty pipeline, decryption/rendering service — was an off-chain ongoing obligation that stopped. Robot Cache is the extreme: downloaded games bricked at shutdown. Decentralizing the record without decentralizing (or escrowing) the fulfillment obligation delivers no real ownership.
findings.source: Synthesis of Massively OP (2026), Forbes (2022), PYMNTS (2024), Chartlex (2026) — cited above

findings.topic: Recurring lesson — speculation-first audiences and crypto stigma
findings.fact: Buyers on NFT content platforms were predominantly speculators; when speculation ended, platforms whose UX was minting/listing/trading had no residual product, while those hiding crypto as invisible rails survived. Simultaneously, leading with NFT branding actively repelled mainstream consumers (Ubisoft's mass-disliked launch; gamer hostility), and 'regulatory uncertainty' (GameStop) plus Howey exposure (Royal) made ownership-with-profit-share legally radioactive in the US through 2024.

research.summary: Two decades of platform-independent ownership attempts split into two families, and both fail for reasons unrelated to their core technology. The consortium family (UltraViolet, 2011-2019) proved a neutral rights registry can work architecturally — Neustar's Coordinator held only entitlement records, so shutdown was survivable via retailer migration — but died because the most powerful rights holder (Disney) refused and built a rival, and the dominant retailers (Apple, Amazon, Google) never integrated. Its winner, Movies Anywhere (14.5M users, five studios, ~10,000 films in 2026), succeeds precisely because it is not neutral: Disney owns it and can compel participation — yet Google's month-long 2025 walkout shows even that interop is a revocable business courtesy. The blockchain family (Robot Cache, GameStop NFT, Ubisoft Quartz, Royal, Sound.xyz, OneOf) added token persistence but never decentralized the fulfillment obligation: Robot Cache's downloaded games bricked at its May 2026 shutdown despite blockchain branding; Ubisoft's Digits persist uselessly on Tezos; Royal hit the Howey wall. Recurring killers, in rough order of lethality: rights-holder non-participation; off-chain fulfillment dying with the company; speculator-only demand (Robot Cache booked $532 revenue in 2024); securities exposure whenever ownership includes profit share; crypto stigma; and consortium governance latency. Survivors (Book.io, Anotherblock, Catalog) are tiny, consumptive-framed, or pushed compliance to specialists; the 2025 wave (Story Protocol) pivoted to B2B programmable IP licensing for AI. Meanwhile regulation (California AB 2426) and consumer politics (Stop Killing Games, 1.45M signatures) are creating genuine tailwinds for verifiable-license infrastructure — the demand is real; every failure was on the supply and fulfillment side.
design_implications: Separate the license registry from content delivery and DRM (UltraViolet's Coordinator model): the registry should store portable entitlement records that multiple independent fulfillers can honor, so the platform's death does not destroy access.

Design end-of-life into the protocol from day one: contractual/technical graceful degradation (key escrow, DRM-free fallback copies, or automatic entitlement migration to other retailers) is the single feature that separates UltraViolet's wind-down from Robot Cache's game-bricking — and it is becoming a regulatory expectation (Stop Killing Games, AB 2426).

Treat rights-holder acquisition as the core product problem, not adoption of the tech: even a 70%-of-resale revenue share (Robot Cache) failed to attract catalogs. Without an anchor tenant with market power or a regulatory mandate, neutral registries stall; plan for indie/backlist/public-domain seeding rather than fighting incumbents for AAA/frontlist content.

Get the retailers/clients, not just the rights holders: UltraViolet had nearly every studio but died because Apple, Amazon and Google refused to integrate. An open registry needs at least one mass-market consumption surface that honors its licenses natively.

Never attach profit expectation to the license token: any fractional-royalty or resale-yield framing triggers Howey/securities analysis (Royal.io's death, GameStop's cited 'regulatory uncertainty'). Keep tokens strictly consumptive (proof-of-license), and route any revenue-share features through regulated specialist partners (the Anotherblock→Polytrade pattern).

Hide the chain: every venture that led with NFT/crypto branding suffered stigma and speculator capture; survivors made blockchain invisible infrastructure (Sound Premium). Wallets and keys must be invisible by default — custodial onboarding with a guaranteed export-to-self-custody path — because mandatory self-custody is a UX killer and pure custody is fake ownership (Robot Cache).

Token persistence ≠ license persistence: an on-chain record is only as valuable as the off-chain obligation it points to. Make the registry entry legally bind the rights holder (a real license grant with survivability terms), not just cryptographically bind a pointer to a service that can vanish.

Store or escrow what's needed to exercise the license independently: Book.io's encrypted-asset approach (token gates decryption of content on decentralized storage) is the most credible survivability architecture observed, but it remains unproven at scale (~130k units, vendor-reported).

Expect the interop layer to be attacked by bilateral business disputes: even Movies Anywhere lost Google for a month in 2025 over an unrelated carriage fight. Protocol design should minimize per-retailer bilateral dependencies (open verification APIs, no per-partner rights silos).

Consider B2B/compliance positioning before consumer marketplace positioning: the only well-capitalized 2025-2026 entrant (Story Protocol) sells programmable IP licensing rails to businesses and AI companies, and AB 2426-style disclosure laws create a compliance market for verifiable license registries — a more defensible wedge than consumer resale, which structurally threatens rights holders' primary sales.

Do not mistake vocal demand for purchase behavior: 1.45M petition signatures and Kindle backlash coexist with $532/year revenue at the most ownership-forward game store. Validate willingness-to-pay for ownership guarantees (resale value, permanence) before building marketplace liquidity features.

Keep governance lightweight: DECE's ~90-member consortium took 2.5 years to ship a spec while Disney shipped a product. An open registry needs a small core spec team with credible neutrality, not committee-of-the-whole governance.

findings.fact: CJEU held that the distribution right in a computer program is exhausted on first sale even for downloaded copies, when the license is perpetual and priced at the copy's economic value; the reseller must render their own copy unusable, cannot split multi-user licenses, cannot transfer maintenance contracts, and time-limited licenses do not trigger exhaustion. The ruling rests on the lex specialis Software Directive 2009/24, not the InfoSoc Directive.
findings.source: CJEU C-128/11 UsedSoft v Oracle, 3 July 2012; Osborne Clarke and Practical Law analyses (uk.practicallaw.thomsonreuters.com/8-520-1725; osborneclarke.com)

findings.topic: Tom Kabinet — no EU exhaustion for non-software content
findings.fact: The CJEU Grand Chamber ruled that supplying an e-book for permanent use is a 'communication to the public' under InfoSoc Directive art 3, not distribution, so exhaustion never arises; resale of e-books requires rightsholder authorization. Commentators expect the same logic to cover audiobooks, music downloads and the non-software elements of video games. This remains good law in 2026 — the Commission cited these exclusive-rights principles when rejecting the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative.
findings.source: CJEU C-263/18 NUV/GAU v Tom Kabinet, 19 December 2019; IPKat (ipkitten.blogspot.com/2019/12/breaking-cjeu-rules-that-provision-of.html); GRUR International 69(5):489

findings.topic: ReDigi — no US first-sale for digital files
findings.fact: The Second Circuit affirmed that a 'used digital music' marketplace infringed the reproduction right: first sale (17 U.S.C. §109(a)) limits only the distribution right, and every digital transfer fixes a new phonorecord even with a delete-on-transfer mechanism; fair use was rejected as commercial, non-transformative resale into the same market. No later US authority has reopened digital first sale.
findings.source: Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc., 910 F.3d 649 (2d Cir., 12 December 2018) (law.justia.com/cases/federal/appellate-courts/ca2/16-2321/16-2321-2018-12-12.html)

findings.topic: California AB 2426 — 'buy' labeling for revocable licenses
findings.fact: Effective 1 January 2025, California bans terms like 'buy'/'purchase' for digital goods sold as revocable licenses unless the seller obtains the consumer's affirmative acknowledgment of the license and its restrictions, or gives a clear-and-conspicuous statement with access to full license terms. Exemptions: subscription-only access, free goods, and goods the seller cannot revoke (e.g., permanent offline downloads). Violation is a misdemeanor with fines up to $2,500 per violation plus UCL/private-action exposure.
findings.source: Cal. AB 2426, signed 24 September 2024 (leginfo.legislature.ca.gov); Sidley Austin (sidley.com, Nov 2024); Morrison Foerster (mofo.com, 18 March 2025)

findings.topic: AB 2426 enforcement — class actions reach game stores
findings.fact: Weber v. GameStop (E.D. Cal. No. 2:26-at-00047, filed January 2026) attacks the entire purchase flow for a digital game — marketing copy, cart mechanics, rewards language — not just the checkout button, under the FAL (AB 2426), UCL and CLRA. Litigation is ongoing; the case signals that disclosure compliance is judged across the whole customer journey.
findings.source: Frankfurt Kurnit Klein & Selz, Focus on Technology Law blog (technologylaw.fkks.com/post/102mdrc), 2026

findings.topic: Copycat laws — Maryland, New York, adjacent state laws
findings.fact: Maryland HB 208 (2025 session) took effect 1 October 2025 and mirrors AB 2426: disclosure or affirmative acknowledgment required when selling revocable digital-good licenses with ownership-implying language; it exempts subscriptions, free goods, and goods permanently downloadable offline to external storage. New York has similar legislation pending. Colorado's HB 25-1090 (effective 1 January 2026) is adjacent but targets junk-fee/total-price disclosure, not license labeling. No non-US equivalent statute was verified; in the EU the issue is being channeled into the Digital Fairness Act instead.
findings.source: Perkins Coie update (perkinscoie.com/insights/update/maryland-imposes-new-requirements-businesses-sell-digital-goods); Maryland HB 208 (mgaleg.maryland.gov/2025rs); Goodwin CFS trends (goodwinlaw.com, Jan 2026)

findings.topic: Stop Killing Games ECI — outcome as of mid-2026
findings.fact: The 'Stop Destroying Videogames' ECI was submitted with 1,294,188 verified statements of support on 26 January 2026, had a European Parliament hearing on 16 April 2026 and a plenary debate on 21 May 2026. On 16 June 2026 the Commission's Communication (C(2026) 4110) declined to propose any legal obligation to keep games playable, citing rightsholders' exclusive IP rights (including the right to set license conditions and termination) and existing remedies under the Digital Content Directive (EU) 2019/770.
findings.source: European Commission, digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu news and C(2026) 4110 (citizens-initiative.europa.eu/stop-destroying-videogames_en), 16 June 2026

findings.topic: Stop Killing Games — Commission commitments and follow-up
findings.fact: Instead of legislation the Commission committed, by end-2026, to: (1) facilitate a voluntary industry code of conduct with publishers and consumer groups on video-game end-of-life practices, (2) consumer-awareness actions, and (3) a report on how the Digital Content Directive applies to service discontinuation. Organizers are now lobbying to fold the demands into the Digital Fairness Act. The UK Parliament had similarly declined legislative change in November 2025.
findings.source: Lewis Silkin insight, 24 June 2026 (lewissilkin.com); EC digital-strategy news, 16 June 2026

findings.topic: Digital Fairness Act — pending EU vehicle for digital-ownership rules
findings.fact: The Commission is expected to table the Digital Fairness Act proposal in Q3 2026, targeting dark patterns, addictive design, unfair personalization, in-game virtual currencies (including possible real-money value labeling) and one-sided digital licensing practices. The public consultation was flooded with responses from gamers urging anti-'game-killing' provisions; whether ownership/persistence rules make the final text is undecided as of July 2026.
findings.source: European Parliament Legislative Train (europarl.europa.eu/legislative-train); Freshfields game-developer guide (technologyquotient.freshfields.com); EFF recommendations (eff.org/deeplinks/2026/04)

findings.topic: EU DMA — applicability to content stores
findings.fact: The DMA binds only designated gatekeepers; Apple is designated for the App Store, iOS, Safari and iPadOS. Apple was fined €500M on 23 April 2025 for anti-steering breaches of art 5(4) and by 2026 must allow links to external purchases; alternative app marketplaces and web distribution operate in the EU, with the per-install Core Technology Fee replaced by a 5% Core Technology Commission from 1 January 2026. The Commission's April 2026 review (COM(2026) 178) concluded the DMA needs no revision. A new decentralized platform would not itself be a gatekeeper but benefits from gatekeepers' openness obligations.
findings.source: European Commission DMA portal and review report COM(2026) 178, 28 April 2026 (digital-markets-act.ec.europa.eu); Perkins Coie on the Apple decision; TechCrunch 26 June 2025; Apple developer support (developer.apple.com/support/dma-and-apps-in-the-eu/)

findings.fact: DMCA §512 offers four safe harbors (conduit, caching, storage at user direction, information-location tools) conditioned on: no actual or red-flag knowledge, expeditious takedown on notice, no direct financial benefit where there is right and ability to control, a registered designated agent, and a repeat-infringer policy. Providers outside the safe harbors face direct or secondary (contributory/vicarious) liability claims. The regime presumes an identifiable operator — anonymous node operators cannot practically satisfy the agent-registration and policy conditions.
findings.source: U.S. Copyright Office §512 resources (copyright.gov/512); CRS report IF11478 (congress.gov)

findings.topic: US secondary liability — Grokster inducement rule
findings.fact: The Supreme Court held unanimously that one who distributes a device or service with the object of promoting copyright infringement, shown by statements or conduct (targeting known infringing demand, refusing filtering while profiting from infringement), is liable for resulting third-party infringement regardless of the technology's lawful capabilities; mere knowledge of infringing uses or ordinary support activities is not enough. This is the doctrine that defeated decentralized P2P architectures despite their lack of central content custody.

The DSA carries over the e-Commerce liability exemptions: art 4 mere conduit (immune when not intervening in transmitted content), art 5 caching, art 6 hosting (liable only with actual knowledge/awareness of illegality and failure to act expeditiously; general awareness that a service could carry illegal content does not create knowledge), and art 8 no general monitoring obligation. These apply horizontally, including to copyright, but are overridden for content-sharing platforms by CDSM art 17.

findings.topic: EU platform liability — CDSM art 17 and CJEU case law
findings.fact: An OCSSP (main purpose: storing and giving public access to large amounts of user-uploaded protected works, organized and promoted for profit) loses the hosting safe harbor and is directly liable unless it obtains authorization or shows best efforts at licensing plus notice-and-staydown; a carve-out exists for services under 3 years old with turnover below €10M. Separately, YouTube/Cyando (C-682/18, 2021) holds platform operators liable for communication to the public when they deliberately contribute beyond mere provision, and Ziggo (C-610/15, 2017) held that even a pure index/tracker that hosts nothing (The Pirate Bay) communicates works to the public when its operators knowingly facilitate infringing access.
findings.source: Directive (EU) 2019/790 art 17; CJEU C-682/18 & C-683/18, 22 June 2021; CJEU C-610/15, 14 June 2017; IPKat and Kluwer Copyright Blog analyses

findings.fact: Under 17 U.S.C. §101 a 'copy' exists if the work is fixed and can be perceived 'with the aid of a machine or device'; the test is technical recoverability, not human readability, so an encrypted unauthorized copy is still an infringing copy, and the EU reproduction right (InfoSoc art 2) covers reproduction 'in any manner or form'. No controlling precedent specifically blessing or condemning encrypted-chunk distribution (IPFS/Storj-style sharding) was found — encryption chiefly affects the host's knowledge (red-flag/awareness) analysis and evidentiary posture, not the existence of infringement. This point is doctrinally solid but litigation-untested for sharded storage; treat as unresolved risk, not safe ground.

Protocol Labs runs a DMCA notice process for its own gateways (ipfs.io, dweb.link) and maintains a 'badbits' denylist returning HTTP 410 for taken-down CIDs; pinning services honor takedowns for their hosted copies, but removal never deletes content from the network, and independent gateways may ignore the denylist. Publishers have mass-mailed DMCA notices to volunteer gateway operators (one received notices covering 7,350 URLs never actually accessed), showing that operators of any resolvable endpoint become the practical enforcement targets.
findings.source: IPFS legal page (ipfs.tech/legal); TorrentFreak, 25 June 2023 (torrentfreak.com/publishers-carpet-bomb-ipfs-gateway-operators-with-dmca-notices-230625/); Techdirt, 7 July 2023

findings.fact: The D.C. Circuit in Green v. DOJ (2 August 2024) upheld §1201's anti-circumvention and anti-trafficking provisions against a facial First Amendment challenge, holding they regulate conduct, not speech; there is no fair-use right to circumvent. The Ninth Triennial rulemaking (final rule effective 28 October 2024) renewed narrow exemptions (repair, accessibility, institutional preservation) but denied the exemption for remote access to preserved video games, and no exemption permits consumers to strip DRM to format-shift their own purchases. The next (tenth) triennial cycle concludes in 2027.
findings.source: Green v. Department of Justice, D.C. Cir., 2 August 2024 (eff.org/cases/green-v-us-department-justice; finnegan.com); 89 Fed. Reg. (28 October 2024) (federalregister.gov/documents/2024/10/28/2024-24563); Video Game History Foundation statement (gamehistory.org/dmca-2024-statement/)

findings.topic: EU anti-circumvention — InfoSoc art 6 and Nintendo v PC Box
findings.fact: InfoSoc art 6 requires legal protection against circumvention of effective TPMs and against trafficking in circumvention means; the art 6(4) mechanism meant to preserve exceptions (like private copying) is weak in practice, so the private-copy exception gives users no enforceable right to break DRM to format-shift purchases. Nintendo v PC Box (C-355/12, 23 January 2014) adds a proportionality limit: TPM protection cannot extend to banning devices with commercially significant non-infringing uses — a shield for multi-purpose tools, not for content-liberation features.
findings.source: Directive 2001/29 art 6 (eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/dir/2001/29/oj); CJEU C-355/12, 23 January 2014 (Lexology; SCL analysis scl.org/3014)

findings.fact: California plaintiffs sued Ubisoft after The Crew's 2024 server shutdown made purchased copies unplayable (FAL, UCL, CLRA, warranty and gift-card-expiry theories); Ubisoft's defense is that buyers received conspicuous notice of a limited license, not ownership. On 31 March 2026 French consumer group UFC-Que Choisir, backed by Stop Killing Games, filed its own suit over deceptive ownership marketing and abusive clauses. No final ruling as of July 2026 — the license-vs-ownership question is live on both continents.
findings.source: PC Gamer (pcgamer.com, April 2025); Yahoo News / Outrun Gaming (March 2026)

research.summary: The mid-2026 landscape splits cleanly. Exhaustion/resale: a statutory digital resale right exists only for software in the EU (UsedSoft, under strict conditions — perpetual license, full-value fee, seller's copy destroyed, no license splitting). Tom Kabinet forecloses exhaustion for all other digital works in the EU, and ReDigi forecloses it entirely in the US, so any resale/transfer feature must be a contractual, rightsholder-authorized license term recorded in a registry — never a legal default. Consumer-labeling law is the fastest-moving front: California AB 2426 (effective 2025-01-01) and Maryland HB 208 (2025-10-01) ban "buy" language for revocable licenses absent disclosures, class actions (Weber v. GameStop, Jan 2026) already attack entire purchase flows, and New York is pending. The Stop Destroying Videogames ECI (1,294,188 verified signatures) was answered on 2026-06-16: no legislation, only a voluntary industry code of conduct by end-2026 and a Digital Content Directive report; organizers pivoted to the Digital Fairness Act (proposal expected Q3 2026). Liability: DSA arts 4-8 and DMCA §512 shield neutral conduits/hosts that run notice-and-takedown, but CDSM art 17 imposes direct liability on for-profit content-sharing platforms, and Grokster, Ziggo and YouTube/Cyando make purpose and knowing facilitation the decisive tests — no architecture routes around inducement, and even a hosting-nothing index can infringe. Encrypted unauthorized copies remain copies (17 U.S.C. §101 machine-aided perceptibility; InfoSoc art 2 "any manner or form"), though sharded-storage liability is litigation-untested. Anti-circumvention law (§1201, upheld in Green v. DOJ; InfoSoc art 6) bars DRM-stripping even of one's own purchases; no format-shifting exemption exists in either system.
design_implications: Separate the registry from the content: an open ledger of licenses/entitlements (metadata, attestations, transfer records) carries near-zero copyright risk; the moment the platform stores, indexes, or gateways the works themselves, the full §512/DSA/art-17 liability stack applies. Keep the registry layer content-free by design.

Resale/transfer must be a rights-holder-granted contractual feature encoded in the license terms the registry records — not a platform default. Only exception: EU software distributed under perpetual, fully-paid licenses can support UsedSoft-compliant transfers (with proof the seller's copy is disabled and no license splitting); ebooks, music, films and game content cannot rely on exhaustion anywhere.

Encryption does not launder infringement: encrypted or sharded unauthorized copies are still copies under US fixation doctrine and the EU reproduction right. The network must gate what enters distribution (only rightsholder-authorized payloads, verified at publish time), not rely on nodes being unable to read what they host.

Every operated choke point (gateway, pinning service, index, search, default node software distribution) needs safe-harbor plumbing: US designated agent, repeat-infringer policy, notice-and-takedown; EU expeditious removal on knowledge (DSA art 6) plus a denylist mechanism nodes actually consume (IPFS badbits shows both the pattern and its limits — takedown never purges the network, and volunteer gateway operators become the enforcement targets).

Avoid the OCSSP profile under CDSM art 17: if the platform's main purpose is giving public access to large amounts of user-uploaded protected content organized and promoted for profit, it is directly liable in the EU unless it licenses and filters. A license-registry with rightsholder-onboarded catalogs (authorization first, distribution second) inverts that risk; note the <3 years / <€10M startup carve-out is temporary.

Inducement is the hard red line no technical design routes around (Grokster in the US; Ziggo/YouTube-Cyando in the EU): marketing, onboarding copy, defaults, and community moderation must never target or celebrate unauthorized use; indexing/curating infringing content creates liability even with zero hosting. Governance documents and public communications are legal-risk surfaces equal to code.

No DRM-stripping or circumvention features, ever — not even 'import your own purchases': §1201 (constitutionality confirmed by Green v. DOJ, 2024) and InfoSoc art 6 prohibit circumvention and trafficking in tools regardless of fair use or private-copy exceptions, and the 2024 triennial granted no personal format-shifting exemption. Ingestion of user libraries must be limited to DRM-free files or publisher-blessed migration APIs.

Label honestly or make it truly irrevocable: if an entitlement can be revoked, AB 2426/Maryland HB 208 forbid 'buy' language without acknowledgment/disclosure, and Weber v. GameStop shows courts examine the entire purchase funnel. The exemption for goods the seller cannot revoke (permanent offline download) is a design opportunity — a registry that makes entitlements technically irrevocable both escapes disclosure liability and matches the regulatory direction.

Build end-of-life persistence into license templates now: the Commission's SKG response creates a voluntary code-of-conduct process by end-2026 and the Digital Fairness Act (Q3 2026 proposal) may harden it. Escrowed offline patches, server-sunset commitments, and license survival clauses are cheap to standardize in a registry schema and likely to become compliance assets.

Use the DMA rather than fear it: a small decentralized platform is nowhere near gatekeeper thresholds, while Apple's EU obligations (alternative marketplaces, web distribution, anti-steering remedies, 5% CTC from 2026) open iOS as a distribution channel for an independent content store in the EU.

Design for identifiable accountability at the edges: fully anonymous node operators cannot claim §512 safe harbors (agent registration, policies) and attract carpet-bomb DMCA campaigns; a two-tier network — accountable operated infrastructure with compliance machinery, plus thin verification-only peers that never relay content — tracks the legal grain.

Jurisdiction-partition the feature set: EU-only UsedSoft software transfers, US/EU-wide contractual transfers where licensed, disclosure flows keyed to buyer state (CA/MD now, NY likely) — the registry schema should carry per-jurisdiction capability flags rather than assuming one global rule.

findings.fact: Legacy Filecoin sealed-sector storage deals are quoted around $0.19/TB/month as of April 2026 (vs ~$23/TB for S3), but this is the archival deal market where data is sealed and retrieval historically took hours to days — unsuitable for interactive content delivery.

findings.topic: Storage — Filecoin Onchain Cloud warm storage
findings.fact: Filecoin Onchain Cloud (launched Nov 18, 2025; warm storage GA Jan 2026) prices warm storage at 2.5 USDFC ($2.50)/TiB/month per copy with a 2-copy minimum (~$5/TiB/mo effective) plus a flat 0.024 USDFC/month per-dataset rail fee; data stays unsealed with PDP (Proof of Data Possession) so it is retrievable without unsealing latency; over 100 teams building, Filecoin Pay has 180 payers and 6,500+ payment rails.

FilCDN (successor to the Saturn project) is the incentivized decentralized CDN layer that caches and serves Filecoin/IPFS data for low-latency retrieval; Storacha, an early Onchain Cloud partner, sells warm verifiable storage at $5.99/TiB/month.

Arweave permanent storage is a one-time payment (endowment modeled on 200+ years) fluctuating roughly $6.35–8.00 per GB, i.e. ~$6.5–8M one-time per PB — viable for small immutable license metadata/manifests, prohibitive for petabyte video masters; price varies with AR token and network dynamics.
findings.source: ardrive.io cost pages + ar-fees.arweave.net + search-aggregated Apr 2026 range

findings.fact: Storj's simplified pricing (new projects from Jul 1, 2026): Standard tier $7/TB/month storage + $7/TB egress; Advanced tier (US SOC2 data centers) $10/TB; minimum monthly fee rises from $5 to $50 on Jul 1, 2026; 50 KB minimum billable object size, 30-day minimum retention, 11-nines durability via erasure coding.

Independent IDS 2025 benchmarks measured Storj up to 16.7x faster cross-region throughput than AWS/Wasabi without replication and 42% faster than AWS on 5MB downloads; parallel erasure-coded segment downloads make throughput improve (+11.5%) as files grow, and Storj explicitly markets/documents 4K video streaming workloads.

Pinata 2026 pricing: free 1GB/500 files; Picnic $20/mo (1TB storage, 500GB bandwidth, dedicated gateway+CDN); Fiesta $100/mo (5TB storage, 2.5TB bandwidth, 3 gateways); overages $0.035–0.07/GB storage and $0.08–0.10/GB bandwidth; HLS video streaming is offered on higher tiers via its gateway/CDN layer.
Filebase paid IPFS plans start at $20/month with 5GB free; data placed on Sia buckets bills at $0.0059/GB (~$5.90/TB) for both storage and bandwidth.

4K streaming (needs ~15–25 Mbps sustained per viewer) from decentralized storage is feasible in 2026 only through a gateway/CDN layer: Storj S3-compatible parallel downloads, Pinata dedicated gateways with HLS, or FilCDN over Filecoin warm storage; streaming raw from public IPFS gateways or Filecoin archival deals is not viable.
findings.source: storj.io performance report 2025 + pinata.cloud/pricing + messari.io FilCDN coverage

findings.fact: Base transactions average under $0.01 in 2026: simple transfers ~$0.002–0.02, complex DeFi interactions up to ~$0.10, fluctuating with L2 congestion and Ethereum blob costs.
findings.source: chaincost.app Base network fees guide + basescan.org fee chart (2026)

findings.fact: Arbitrum One average fee was ~$0.0044 in April 2026 after the ArbOS 'Dia' upgrade (Jan 8, 2026) introduced a multi-gas-target EIP-1559-style base-fee controller; under load, snapshot trackers show ~$0.09 to send ETH and ~$0.27 for a swap.

Solana base fee is 5,000 lamports (0.000005 SOL) per signature; average all-in fee runs ~$0.00025–0.0006 with priority fees typically under $0.01 even at peak; the Firedancer validator client reached mainnet in December 2025 and smoothed congestion-driven fee spikes.
findings.source: solana.com/docs/core/fees + tokenterminal.com Solana avg fee + stakepoint.app 2026 guide

findings.topic: Chain costs — Polygon and Ethereum L1
findings.fact: Polygon PoS averages ~$0.002 per transaction in 2026; Ethereum L1 by comparison costs ~$1.10 to send ETH and ~$5.48 for a swap in a mid-2026 snapshot, so L1 remains unsuitable for high-frequency registry writes.

Rollup-as-a-Service mainnet chains start around $3,000/month on Conduit ($50/mo testnet); Caldera and others quote privately, with cost drivers being sequencer redundancy, DA layer choice and support tier — so a purpose-built L2 only pays off with sustained volume or sovereignty requirements, not for registry writes that cost fractions of a cent on shared L2s.

By 2026 industry trackers estimate ~2.4 billion cumulative ERC-4337 UserOperations, ~62 million active smart accounts on EVM chains (Coinbase Smart Wallet and Safe leading), and ~14 million EOAs that signed at least one EIP-7702 authorization since Pectra activated it on mainnet May 7, 2025; MetaMask, Rabby and Trust ship 7702 support (figures are secondary-source estimates).

Smart accounts now validate WebAuthn signatures via the secp256r1 precompile path, so a phone passkey (Face ID / Windows Hello) can be the wallet signer directly; passkey sign-in, social recovery, gas sponsorship, stablecoin gas payment and batching are standard smart-wallet features in 2026.

Embedded-wallet SDKs (Privy — acquired by Stripe June 2025, Coinbase CDP Embedded Wallets with MPC keys in secure enclaves, Openfort, Magic, Web3Auth, Alchemy, Circle, Particle, Fireblocks) let consumers onboard with email/social/passkey and hold NFTs non-custodially with no seed phrase; MPC gives non-custodial guarantees with custodial-grade UX, so a normal consumer holding an NFT license without seed-phrase risk is production reality in 2026.

Lit Protocol's v1 mainnet 'Naga' went live after a Dec 17, 2025 DKG with 7 node operators and the LITKEY TGE (Q4 2025); the prior beta secured >$150M in assets and processed millions of signing/decryption requests monthly — but Naga is already being sunset (operations cease Mar 25, 2026, full shutdown Apr 1, 2026) in favor of Lit v3 'Chipotle', a ground-up rebuild unifying confidential compute and key management, with production targeted Mar 25, 2026; v0 Datil shut down Feb 25, 2026.
findings.source: spark.litprotocol.com/v1-live + spark.litprotocol.com/naga-network-sunset + messari.io Lit Protocol report

findings.fact: TACo (Threshold/NuCypher lineage) offers open-source threshold access control positioned as the decentralized alternative to Lit, with drop-in migration docs from Lit and integrations with Codex, Waku and ComposeDB; however, the original TACo project is expected to be forked and relaunched under the World Ethical Data Forum in H2 2026, so it too carries governance-transition risk.

The pattern 'content encrypted at rest, DEK released by a threshold network only when an on-chain license condition is met' is deployed at production scale (millions of monthly decryption requests on Lit alone), but the leading provider has cycled through three incompatible network generations (Datil → Naga → v3) between late 2025 and mid 2026, making provider abstraction and key-rotation strategy mandatory.

ERC-5564 stealth addresses are standardized and deployable at the wallet layer today; Umbra has generated over 77,000 active stealth addresses, and in February 2026 the Ethereum ecosystem outlined a push to roll out ERC-5564 support broadly so payments/receipts stay unlinkable without mixers.
findings.source: eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-5564 + BingX/U.Today coverage of the Feb 18, 2026 Ethereum privacy roadmap

findings.fact: Proving 'I hold a valid license' without revealing the buying wallet is possible today with app-level ZK circuits, and Aztec (privacy L2, alpha network in 2026) runs private functions client-side in the browser PXE with draft private-token standards AIP-20 and AIP-721 — usable for private NFT ownership but still alpha-stage, not yet boring infrastructure.
findings.source: aztec.network blog (Alpha Build 2) + coingecko.com/learn Aztec explainer + chainscorelabs ZK/NFT analyses

findings.fact: On Base/Arbitrum/Polygon/Solana, every license purchase is public and linkable by default; the deployable 2026 mitigations are stealth addresses for receipt unlinkability plus off-chain/ZK verification of ownership, while fully private registries (Aztec-style) remain pre-production.

ERC-5218 (NFT Rights Management) remains the reference interface for on-chain license trees — a root license bound to the NFT owner, sublicenses, transfer and revocation, dual human-readable/machine-readable (e.g. CC REL) terms, ERC-721-compatible — with ERC-5554 (legal use/remixing metadata) and ERC-5635 (licensing-agreement oracle for derivatives) extending it; no successor standard has displaced it as of 2026, and a 2025 EU Blockstand paper on standardizing on-chain IP rights still builds on it.

Story Protocol (dedicated IP L1 with the Programmable IP License — an off-chain US-copyright legal document with on-chain-enforced parameters, still the only license template on the protocol) hard-forked Jan 14, 2026 to add programmable licensing features, then rebranded as The DATA Foundation, pivoting from general IP licensing to AI-training-data provenance (Trace); token unlocks were delayed to Aug 13, 2026.
findings.source: docs.story.foundation PIL docs + Benzinga press release Feb 2026 + datawallet.com Story explainer

findings.fact: ERC-2981 royalty info is widely adopted but purely declarative; since OpenSea ended its Operator Filter in 2023, enforcement is fragmented across on-chain-enforced, UI-policy, and royalty-ignoring marketplaces, so a license business cannot rely on marketplace royalty enforcement in 2026.
findings.source: eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-2981 + calmops.com NFT marketplace architecture 2026 + onekey.so ERC-2981 explainer

findings.fact: ERC-6551 token-bound accounts (an NFT that owns its own wallet/assets) is live and useful for attaching payment history, sublicense revenue or renewal state directly to a license NFT; ERC-1155 remains the fit for high-volume multi-edition licenses while ERC-721 fits unique/exclusive grants.

research.summary: The 2026 stack for a decentralized license registry plus content network is viable, but only with a layered architecture. Storage economics split cleanly by role: Filecoin Onchain Cloud warm storage (~$2.50/TiB/mo per copy, 2-copy minimum, PDP-verified, launched Jan 2026) and Storj ($7/TB/mo + $7/TB egress, $50/mo minimum from July 2026) handle petabyte media at $5-10k/PB/month, while Arweave (~$6.35-8/GB one-time) is only sane for small immutable license metadata, never video masters. 4K streaming works today, but exclusively through gateway/CDN layers (Storj's benchmarked parallel downloads, Pinata dedicated gateways with HLS at $20-100/mo tiers, FilCDN over Filecoin) — never raw IPFS gateways. Registry writes are effectively free everywhere: Base <$0.01, Arbitrum ~$0.004 (post-Dia), Polygon ~$0.002, Solana ~$0.0005; a $3,000+/month Conduit-style appchain only makes sense for sovereignty, not cost. Wallet UX is solved: 62M+ active smart accounts, EIP-7702 live since Pectra (May 2025), passkey signers via secp256r1, and Stripe-owned Privy / Coinbase CDP embedded wallets mean consumers can hold NFT licenses without seed phrases. Token-gated decryption is production-scale but churn-prone — Lit cycled Datil→Naga→v3 in six months (Naga sunset April 1, 2026) and TACo faces a H2 2026 fork — so abstract the key layer. Privacy: stealth addresses (ERC-5564, Umbra 77k+ addresses) deploy today; ZK ownership proofs are buildable; Aztec remains alpha. No license standard won: ERC-5218 is still the reference, Story Protocol pivoted away to AI data, and ERC-2981 royalties are unenforceable — design your own enforcement.
design_implications: Tier storage by role: license records and legal-text hashes on-chain + Arweave (one-time ~$7/GB is trivial for KB-scale metadata); video masters on Filecoin warm storage (~$5/TiB/mo for 2 copies) or Storj ($7/TB/mo); hot delivery through a CDN/gateway layer — budget roughly $5-10k/month per PB stored plus $7/TB egress-equivalent for delivery.

Treat 4K streaming as a CDN problem, not a storage problem: serve HLS/DASH renditions through Storj S3 endpoints, Pinata dedicated gateways, or FilCDN; never point players at public IPFS gateways; content-address (CID) everything so the delivery layer stays swappable.

Put the registry on a general-purpose L2 (Base or Arbitrum: sub-cent writes, EVM tooling, 4337/7702 support) or Solana if throughput dominates; defer any purpose-built appchain (>$3k/month RaaS baseline + ops burden) until write volume or governance sovereignty actually demands it.

Design wallet onboarding around embedded wallets + passkeys from day one: ERC-4337 smart accounts with WebAuthn/secp256r1 signers, gas sponsorship via paymaster so buyers never hold ETH, social/passkey recovery instead of seed phrases; pick an embedded-wallet vendor (Privy/Stripe, Coinbase CDP) but keep key-export/portability as a hard requirement to avoid lock-in.

For encrypted-at-rest content, use threshold access control (Lit v3 or TACo) but wrap it behind your own DEK-envelope abstraction: per-content data keys wrapped by the threshold network, so you can rotate providers (Lit's three network generations in six months and TACo's pending 2026 fork prove churn is the norm) without re-encrypting the media corpus.

Do not rely on decryption gating alone for enforcement: once decrypted, content can be copied; combine threshold gating with watermarking/forensic marking and legal license terms, and treat the crypto layer as access control, not DRM.

Bake purchase privacy in at the protocol level: support ERC-5564 stealth addresses for license receipt so buyers' collections are unlinkable, and design the license-verification API to accept a ZK proof of ownership (not a raw wallet address) so verifiers never learn the buyer's full on-chain history; keep fully-private Aztec-style deployment as a later phase, it is still alpha.

Model the license as ERC-721 (exclusive/unique grants) or ERC-1155 (multi-edition), implementing an ERC-5218-style license tree (root license, sublicenses, revocation state) with dual machine-readable + hashed legal text, PIL-style; there is no dominant finalized license ERC in 2026, so publish your interface as an open spec to become a Schelling point.

Do not build revenue assumptions on ERC-2981 marketplace royalty enforcement — it is declarative and widely bypassed; enforce economics in your own transfer logic, renewal/subscription state (ERC-6551 token-bound accounts can hold per-license state), or primary-sale margins.

Learn from Story Protocol's pivot to AI-data provenance: general-purpose on-chain IP licensing hasn't found product-market fit as a standalone chain, but AI-training-data licensing is the demand wedge in 2026 — consider machine-readable license terms (usable-for-training flags, provenance attestations) as a first-class registry feature.

Denominate storage and license payments in stablecoins (USDFC on Filecoin, USDC on L2s) — the 2026 stack (Filecoin Pay, paymasters accepting stablecoin gas) supports this end-to-end, insulating both creators and the availability network from token volatility.

findings.fact: The claim is real and very recent: Sony Interactive Entertainment officially announced that physical disc production for NEW PlayStation games ends in January 2028; games released on disc before that date are unaffected. Sony cited consumer preference shifting to digital ('significantly outpaces physical discs'). Note the scope: this covers PlayStation game discs, not all Sony Blu-ray manufacturing.
findings.source: PlayStation Blog, https://blog.playstation.com/2026/07/01/physical-disc-production-ending-in-january-2028-for-new-games-releasing-on-playstation-consoles/ (confirmed by Push Square, Media Play News, Gamefile)

findings.fact: Announced the same day as the disc decision: PlayStation Store on PS3 and PS Vita closes in phases — Mexico/Honduras/Nicaragua August 2026, more LatAm/Middle East regions late 2026, all remaining countries July 2027. Previously purchased content stays downloadable 'for the foreseeable future'. Sony attempted the same closure in March 2021 and reversed it after backlash; this time it is proceeding.

CD Projekt sold 100% of GOG to co-founder Michał Kiciński on Dec 29, 2025 for PLN 90.7M (~$25.2M / ~€21M). Kiciński frames the buyout as 'doubling down' on games that 'truly belong to players, forever', keeping DRM-free as the core tenet, expanding the catalog, exploring Linux support, and partnering with independent studios; CDPR signed a distribution agreement to keep releasing its games on GOG.
findings.source: CD Projekt press release https://www.cdprojekt.com/en/media/news/cd-projekt-co-founder-michal-kicinski-acquires-gog-from-cd-projekt/ + GOG blog + TechRaptor

findings.fact: GOG is mission-strong but margin-thin: net profitability was ~0.6% in 2024 and -0.9% in H1 2025, versus ~35% for the CD Projekt group. In CDPR's FY2025 report GOG appears as a discontinued operation; GOG's operating profit plus the sale proceeds added 74M PLN to group net result. This means GOG competes on trust and differentiation, not cash reserves.
findings.source: CD Projekt FY2025 investor materials via gameworldobserver.com (2026-03-20) and GOG.com Wikipedia

findings.fact: Launched Nov 13, 2024 with ~100 games; 267 games and 1,461 preservation improvements delivered across 2024-2025; still adding waves in 2026 (e.g., The Wolf Among Us, The Last Express in Feb 2026). Key policy: preserved games keep working and receive tech support even if delisted from the store — tested immediately when Microsoft/Blizzard delisted Warcraft 1 & 2 weeks after launch and GOG committed to keep owners' copies working. Partnership with EFGAMP (European game archives federation) since Jan 2025. GOG's internal research found only ~30% of its ~11,000-game catalog was fully compatible with modern systems; preservation is funded from storefront fees at no cost to publishers, and GOG reports more publishers signing on.
findings.source: gog.com/blog/the-gog-preservation-program, gog.com pressroom, gamedeveloper.com 'GOG lays out the business case for robust game preservation', PC Gamer, gamingonlinux.com (2026-02)

findings.fact: GOG Dreamlist (community voting for games to bring back) drew over 13 million votes and 57,000 stories in 2025, with 312 games added to the catalog through it — quantified evidence of consumer demand for restored access to lost titles.
findings.source: GOG Pressroom 'Making games live forever – together', gog.com

findings.fact: California AB 2426 (signed Sept 24, 2024, effective Jan 1, 2025) bans selling digital goods with 'buy/purchase' wording unless the seller conspicuously discloses that the buyer gets a revocable license. Valve pre-empted it in Oct 2024 by adding 'A purchase of a digital product grants a license for the product on Steam' at checkout, generating a large press/community wave about not owning games.
findings.source: Lexology (Steam checkout disclosure analysis), mobilesyrup.com 2024-10-11, techdirt.com 2024-10-17

findings.fact: GOG immediately capitalized on Steam's disclaimer with a viral counter-message: its offline installers 'cannot be taken away from you', explicitly marketing ownership as its differentiator against Steam. This shows the storefront itself treats 'you own what you buy' as its primary competitive wedge.

itch.io passed 200,000 games (Sept 2024), is open-publishing and DRM-free by default, and lets creators set the platform's revenue share themselves (default 10%, can be 0%). It is the largest open indie marketplace and a natural second games partner.
findings.source: itch.io blog 'There are 200,000 games on itch.io' + pcgamesinsider.biz

findings.fact: In July 2025 itch.io deindexed all adult/NSFW content under pressure from payment processors (Visa/Mastercard/PayPal) after the Collective Shout campaign, permanently removing some pages and adding processor-compliance gates for creators. Demonstrates that even independent DRM-free platforms are hostage to centralized payment rails — a governance risk any registry must design around.
findings.source: itch.io official update https://itch.io/updates/update-on-nsfw-content, gamedeveloper.com, aftermath.site, PC Gamer

findings.fact: Songtradr acquired Bandcamp from Epic in Oct 2023 and laid off ~50% of staff (editorial gutted to 3 people), but the core artist-first model survived: 15% digital / 10% physical revenue share retained, DRM-free downloads retained, Bandcamp Daily and Bandcamp Fridays retained.

Bandcamp Friday is alive with 8 dates scheduled in 2026; the Feb 6, 2026 edition raised $3.6M for artists in 24 hours; cumulative Bandcamp Friday payouts hit $154M since 2020 with $19M in 2025 alone. Bandcamp remains the proof that DRM-free paid downloads are a functioning business in music.
findings.source: Music Business Worldwide, digitalmusicnews.com 2026-02-11, Bandcamp Help Center

findings.fact: Under Ziff Davis (owner since 2017), Humble has moved away from ownership-friendly positioning: formerly DRM-free Humble Choice bonus games now require a proprietary launcher with Mac/Linux support dropped, default charity splits were reduced, a 2025 Canadian price hike caused backlash, and Trustpilot sits around 2.6/5. Its DRM-free catalog remains larger than Fanatical/GMG but smaller than GOG. Humble is a weak credibility anchor for an ownership standard.
findings.source: Famiboards thread on Humble Choice launcher, Humble Bundle Wikipedia, tech-insider.org comparison, PCGamingWiki DRM-free list

findings.fact: In Dec 2023 Sony told PlayStation users that over 1,300 seasons of purchased Discovery TV content would be deleted from their libraries on Dec 31, citing licensing arrangements; after massive backlash Sony reversed on Dec 21, securing a license to keep the content. The episode became the canonical example of 'purchased' video vanishing.

Sony shut Funimation on April 2, 2024, migrating users to Crunchyroll; redeemed digital copies (bundled with purchased DVDs/Blu-rays) did NOT transfer and codes became unredeemable, and 150+ series (~60,000 minutes) never appeared on Crunchyroll. Compensation was ad-hoc and inconsistent. Demonstrates entitlement loss during platform consolidation even for disc-linked digital rights.
findings.source: playstationlifestyle.net 2024-02-08, help.crunchyroll.com 'Funimation End of Services', CBR analysis

findings.fact: Ubisoft delisted The Crew (Dec 2023) and shut its servers (April 2024), making even physical copies unplayable. California plaintiffs (Cassell & Liu, filed Nov 2024) allege consumers were told they were buying a game when they were renting a license; packaging showed activation codes valid to 2099. Ubisoft's defense: buyers should not expect to own games forever. French consumer group UFC-Que Choisir filed a parallel suit (March 2025) backed by Stop Killing Games. The case is the live legal test of digital 'ownership' framing.

Sony took Concord offline on Sept 6, 2024, two weeks after launch (~25,000 units sold), and issued full refunds to all buyers including physical copies — establishing an industry precedent that a dead always-online game entails refund liability.
findings.source: PlayStation Blog 'An important update on Concord' 2024-09-03, Variety, Game World Observer

findings.fact: Robot Cache, the blockchain-based PC game store that pioneered digital game resale, announced closure in April 2026 with ~30 days' notice; all purchased games become unplayable even if installed, because license verification depended on the store's own servers. A single-company 'ownership' registry died and took every entitlement with it — the strongest possible argument that a license registry must be independent of any one storefront's survival.

Amazon removed the Kindle 'Download & Transfer via USB' option on Feb 26, 2025, eliminating the last practical path for users to hold local copies of purchased ebooks outside Amazon's ecosystem (and the main DRM-stripping route). Fresh, widely-covered pain point in the books vertical.

The 'Stop Destroying Videogames' ECI was submitted Jan 26, 2026 with 1,294,188 verified signatures. On June 16, 2026 the European Commission declined to propose a legal obligation to keep games playable (citing IP rights) but committed to initiate, by end of 2026, an industry + consumer process to draw up a code of conduct on game 'end of life', plus consumer-rights awareness work. SKG is pivoting to amending the EU Digital Fairness Act. A voluntary industry standard is now explicitly what the EU has asked for — an open license registry maps directly onto that ask.
findings.source: European Commission, https://citizens-initiative.europa.eu/stop-destroying-videogames_en (Communication C_2026_4110), Lewis Silkin analysis 2026-06-24, Stop Killing Games Wikipedia

findings.fact: US consumer spending on DVD/Blu-ray/UHD discs was ~$870M in 2025, down 9.3% YoY — but the decline sharply decelerated from 20%+ drops in 2023-24, and 4K UHD Blu-ray spending GREW ~12% in 2025, driven by collectors and Gen Z (Criterion Collection confirms youth-driven growth). Physical is stabilizing as a niche ownership/collector format, not recovering as mass market — digital 'ownership' substitutes are the growth space.
findings.source: FlatpanelsHD 'UHD Blu-ray returned to growth in 2025' (2026-02), Slashdot 2026-02-25, statsignificant.com

findings.fact: The strongest peer-reviewed evidence remains Perzanowski & Hoofnagle's 'What We Buy When We Buy Now' (U. Penn. Law Review; ~1,300-person representative sample): a large majority of consumers wrongly believe 'Buy Now' confers physical-style ownership rights (keep/lend/resell/use on any device), they value those rights, will pay more for them, and shift to piracy when denied. For 2024-2026, quantitative surveys are thin — I could NOT verify a widely-circulated '40% lost access to purchased content' figure to a primary source; behavioral proxies are stronger: 1.29M SKG signatures, GOG Dreamlist's 13M votes, retro/physical resurgence, and MIDiA Research's qualitative finding of a 'deep hunger' for transferable ownership.
findings.source: Perzanowski & Hoofnagle, U. Penn. Law Review (via boingboing.net summary); midiaresearch.com blog on game ownership; unverified stat flagged explicitly

findings.fact: DRM-free distribution is already normalized at the indie/mid-size tier across verticals: itch.io's 200k games are DRM-free by default; GOG reports growing publisher sign-off on its Preservation Program because it costs publishers nothing (funded from store fees); Tor/Forge (Macmillan's SFF imprint) has sold all ebooks DRM-free since 2012 and publicly confirmed no harm to sales, a policy still in force; Bandcamp's entire catalog is DRM-free downloads with labels participating; all major music download stores dropped DRM in 2009. Resistance is concentrated in AAA games (Blizzard delisting Warcraft from GOG), Big-5 book publishers, and Hollywood studios.
findings.source: gamedeveloper.com (GOG business case), Tor Books Wikipedia + thebookseller.com, itch.io/docs/general/about, Bandcamp Help Center

findings.fact: Movies Anywhere (Disney-operated cross-retailer locker) is the only live large-scale 'buy once, use anywhere' system: ~10,000 movies, 14.5M users averaging ~30 films each, retailers including Prime Video, Apple TV, Fandango at Home, Google/YouTube; Lionsgate joined June 2026 (5 studios now; Paramount/MGM still out). But it is a closed studio consortium, and in Oct-Nov 2025 Google abruptly withdrew during the YouTube TV/Disney carriage dispute, breaking cross-retailer access for a month — showing even the flagship interop system is hostage to any single member's business disputes.
findings.source: Deadline (Lionsgate joins, 2026-05-28), moviesanywhere.com/participants, Movies Anywhere Wikipedia, Media Play News

findings.fact: UltraViolet, the prior industry digital-locker consortium (DECE, every major studio except Disney), shut down July 31, 2019 despite 30M users and 300M stored titles, after studios defected to Movies Anywhere. Lesson: consortium lockers governed by rights holders dissolve when member incentives shift, stranding users on whichever retailer they last linked.

In games, no open cross-store entitlement system exists: Xbox Play Anywhere is single-vendor cross-buy; GOG Galaxy 2.0 aggregates libraries read-only; blockchain attempts (Robot Cache, Ubisoft Quartz, GameStop NFT marketplace) are all dead or defunct, with Robot Cache's 2026 collapse actively destroying user entitlements. The interop vacuum in games is total — which is both the opportunity and the evidence that a neutral registry (not a store, not a token) is the missing primitive.
findings.source: massivelyop.com (Robot Cache), MIDiA Research blog (blockchain demand signal), Movies Anywhere comparison

research.summary: The 2024-2026 evidence is unusually one-directional: digital "ownership" failures are accelerating (Discovery near-wipe 2023, Funimation entitlement loss 2024, The Crew shutdown and lawsuits, Concord refunds, Kindle export removal Feb 2025, PS3/Vita store closures announced July 1, 2026, Robot Cache destroying all purchases May 2026), regulators have responded with disclosure duties rather than ownership rights (California AB 2426 effective Jan 2025; EU Commission on June 16, 2026 declined legislation for Stop Killing Games' 1.29M signatures but ordered an industry end-of-life code of conduct by end 2026), and the user-cited Sony claim is verified real: PlayStation disc production for new games ends January 2028, announced July 1, 2026. Meanwhile the DRM-free economy is healthy at the indie/mid tier: GOG (newly independent under co-founder Kiciński, $25M buyout, Dec 2025) runs a de facto proto-registry via its Preservation Program (267 games, delisting-proof commitments); itch.io hosts 200k DRM-free games; Bandcamp under Songtradr still pays out ($19M via Bandcamp Fridays in 2025); Tor has sold DRM-free ebooks since 2012. Video is closed off — interop belongs to studio consortia (Movies Anywhere, 14.5M users; UltraViolet's 30M-user corpse as warning). The credible v1 wedge is PC games with DRM-free-native storefronts — GOG as anchor partner, itch.io second, plus indie/mid-size publishers for whom a portable license record costs nothing. Every failed predecessor (UltraViolet, Robot Cache) died because entitlements lived inside one company; the registry's core design mandate is to be the thing that survives its members.
design_implications: Wedge choice: v1 = PC games vertical, anchored on GOG (newly independent, founder-owned, mission-aligned, thin margins force differentiation-by-trust, already operates delisting-proof preservation commitments and markets 'installers that cannot be taken away') with itch.io as second partner (200k DRM-free games, creator-set economics). Both need a standards story they cannot credibly build alone after Robot Cache proved single-company registries die with the company. Console and AAA are closed (Blizzard delisted Warcraft from GOG's program); video is locked inside the Movies Anywhere consortium; music/books are follow-on verticals, not v1.

Survivability is the product: every prior system failed by coupling entitlement verification to one company's servers (Robot Cache 2026: installed games bricked; UltraViolet 2019: consortium dissolved). License records must be user-exportable, cryptographically signed, verifiable offline, and replicated across independent registry nodes — the registry must outlive any storefront, including its founders.

A license without an artifact is worthless: pair every registry entry with a guaranteed re-download right for a DRM-free artifact (GOG offline-installer model), ideally with content hashes and mirror obligations across member stores. This is what distinguishes the registry from Steam's 'license on Steam' disclaimer.

Regulatory tailwind timing: AB 2426 makes honest ownership language a compliance problem for stores; the EU Commission explicitly requested a voluntary industry code of conduct on game end-of-life by end 2026. Position the registry as the reference technical implementation of that code — engage the Commission process and consumer groups (UFC-Que Choisir, SKG's Digital Fairness Act push) in 2026 while the window is open.

Publisher economics must be zero-marginal-cost: GOG got publishers to sign preservation commitments because it costs them nothing (funded from store fees). Fund the registry from storefront/platform membership, never per-title publisher fees; for indie publishers already shipping DRM-free on GOG/itch/Humble, joining should be a metadata flag, not an integration project.

Governance must be neutral and censorship-resistant by design: itch.io's July 2025 payment-processor capitulation shows independent platforms get coerced through payment rails. Keep the registry content-neutral (it records licenses, it does not host or sell), so it cannot become — or be captured as — a chokepoint.

Do not use blockchain framing: the demand signal MIDiA saw in blockchain gaming is real, but every implementation (Robot Cache, Ubisoft Quartz, GameStop NFT) failed and burned consumer trust. Ship boring, auditable, signed-receipt infrastructure; the pitch is 'your receipt outlives the store', not tokens or resale (resale triggers publisher resistance — defer it).

Ride the news cycle: Sony's January 2028 disc cutoff (announced 2026-07-01) plus PS3/Vita store closures give an ~18-month narrative arc where 'physical is ending and digital is only a license' is mainstream news. Launch messaging and partner announcements should sync to these dates and to Bandcamp Friday / GOG Preservation waves for earned media.

Design the demand-side loop: GOG Dreamlist (13M votes) and SKG (1.29M signatures) prove organized consumer energy exists; the registry should expose cross-store demand signals (which lost/delisted titles users hold licenses for) as a service to publishers — turning preservation demand into re-release revenue, mirroring GOG's business case.

Books and music as v2, with named beachheads: music is already DRM-free (Bandcamp/labels — lowest friction, lowest urgency); books have fresh pain (Kindle export removal Feb 2025) and a proven DRM-free mid-size publisher precedent (Tor since 2012) but Big-5/Amazon lock-in blocks scale. Sequence: games → music (Bandcamp-style labels) → DRM-free book publishers; treat Hollywood video as out of scope until the registry has leverage.

findings.fact: The Video Game History Foundation and Software Preservation Network study 'Survey of the Video Game Reissue Market in the United States' found that 87% of classic games released in the US before 2010 are out of print; only 13% of a random sample of 1,500 titles (drawn from 4,000+) remained commercially available in any form.

Availability is worst on older platforms: as of April 2023 only about 5.9% of Game Boy games, 4.5% of Commodore 64 games and 12% of PlayStation 2 games were in print — comparable to survival rates of American silent films (~14%) and pre-WWII audio recordings (~10%).

No updated availability percentage has been published since 2023; VGHF's practical follow-up was launching its free Digital Library (Jan 30, 2025) with 30,000+ files — magazines, press assets, development documents, not playable games — which roughly doubled in size during 2025. This shows what a preservation org can lawfully distribute online: contextual materials, not game binaries.

In the ninth Section 1201 triennial rulemaking (final rule Oct 25, 2024, effective Oct 28, 2024), the Librarian of Congress, on the Register's recommendation, denied the SPN/VGHF petition to let libraries and archives circumvent copy protection to provide remote digital access to out-of-print games; the Register accepted the Entertainment Software Association's argument that remote access could become a de facto free 'online arcade' harming the classic-game reissue market.

Per VGHF's statement, the ESA said it would never support remote game access for research under any conditions; VGHF called on individual industry members to pressure the trade group, signalling that voluntary industry cooperation, not rulemaking, is its next avenue.

findings.topic: DMCA — what remains legal in the US
findings.fact: The renewed Section 1201 exemptions still allow libraries, archives and museums to preserve lawfully acquired video games (including games whose servers were discontinued and necessary console jailbreaking for preservation), but access is effectively limited to institutional premises; individual play of server-discontinued games one lawfully owns is also exempt.

The tenth triennial Section 1201 proceeding (2027 cycle) opened in June 2026 with a notice of inquiry and petition window; existing game-preservation exemptions are up for streamlined renewal covering October 2027–October 2030, meaning US preservation permissions remain temporary and must be re-won every three years.

The 'Stop Destroying Videogames' European Citizens' Initiative was formally submitted to the Commission on January 26, 2026 with 1,294,188 verified statements of support (~89% of raw signatures surviving verification), followed by a joint IMCO/JURI/PETI European Parliament hearing on April 16, 2026 and a plenary debate on May 21, 2026.

In its Communication of June 16, 2026, the European Commission declined to propose any legal obligation to keep games playable after commercial withdrawal, citing proportionality and IP rights; instead it committed to convene industry and consumer representatives by end-2026 to draft a voluntary code of conduct on video game 'end of life', to run consumer-awareness work, and to report on application of the Digital Content Directive.

After the Commission's refusal, the campaign pivoted to lobbying for game-preservation amendments to the EU Digital Fairness Act during committee review; by late June 2026 about 45 MEPs had signed an inquiry calling for legislative action, with any amendment needing to survive trilogue through 2026-2027.

findings.topic: Hachette v Internet Archive — final outcome
findings.fact: The Second Circuit affirmed on September 4, 2024 that Internet Archive's controlled digital lending (one-to-one lending of scanned in-copyright books) is not fair use; IA chose not to seek Supreme Court review before the December 3, 2024 deadline, making the case final, and operates under a consent judgment with a permanent injunction covering plaintiffs' commercially available books plus an undisclosed payment to publishers.

The ruling rejects CDL as a fair-use theory in the Second Circuit at least for books with active commercial ebook markets; IA continues operating, honors publisher takedown requests under its AAP agreement, and now pursues legislative/advocacy routes for library digital ownership rather than litigation.

The major labels' (UMG, Sony, Concord) copyright suit over the Great 78 Project (digitized 78-rpm records streamed online) ended in a confidential settlement: a joint notice was filed in the Northern District of California on September 15, 2025, with stipulated dismissal with prejudice to follow once settlement terms are performed — no fair-use precedent was set, but it demonstrates litigation risk even for obsolete-format heritage media.

France is one of the few countries whose legal deposit covers video games: publishers of multimedia works have been obliged to deposit with the BnF since 1992; the collection exceeds 22,000 titles and grows by roughly 1,000 games/year, covers physical media and dematerialized formats plus web archiving of game-related sites, is preserved via emulation, and is consultable by accredited researchers in the research library (a curated selection is playable in the public library) — deposit enables preservation and controlled on-site access, not public online access.

The UK Legal Deposit Libraries (Non-Print Works) Regulations 2013 were not designed for interactive media: works consisting predominantly of film/recorded sound and pure streaming sites are excluded, and video games in practice fall outside legal deposit — a recognized gap that prompted a 2019 multi-institution effort (BFI, V&A, Science Museum Group, British Library, legal deposit libraries, National Videogame Museum) on preserving UK videogame history.

The US has no legal-deposit mandate for video games: LoC's National Audio-Visual Conservation Center holds 5,000+ games acquired via copyright-registration deposits and donations, and works published only online are subject to deposit solely on specific Copyright Office demand under 17 U.S.C. 407(d) — streaming-only and digital-only content is not systematically captured.

In Valancourt Books v. Garland the D.C. Circuit held that the Section 407 mandatory physical deposit requirement, as applied without compensation, is an unconstitutional taking — a signal that expanding US deposit mandates to new media classes faces constitutional headwinds, not just political ones.
findings.source: https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/LSB11043 (CRS Legal Sidebar on the D.C. Circuit ruling)

findings.fact: Article 6 of Directive (EU) 2019/790 (CDSM) obliges all member states to allow cultural heritage institutions to copy works permanently in their collections in any format or medium for preservation; recitals expressly permit institutions to rely on third parties acting on their behalf — including cross-border ones — for making the copies, 'permanent collection' includes legal-deposit holdings, and contractual clauses overriding the exception are unenforceable (Art. 7).

Embracer Games Archive (Karlstad, Sweden), a corporate physical archive, remained active through 2025 (e.g., 500+ games added in April 2025, large-scale sorting underway, plans to open its database to outside queries) and is a member of EFGAMP, the European Federation of Game Archives, Museums and Preservation projects — but as a company-funded archive its continuity is tied to the corporate parent's fortunes.
